title_en: The Fourth and Fifth Pillars: Belief in the Messengers and the Last Day
content_en:
Al-Uthaymin addresses the fourth pillar — belief in the messengers — by first establishing the necessity of prophethood. Since human reason is limited and human beings are inclined to follow desires, Allah in His mercy sent messengers to convey guidance. He notes that the messengers were chosen by Allah, not self-appointed, and were given divine protection from major sins in the fulfilment of their mission (though not from the full range of human experiences, as they were men who ate, slept, fell ill, and died).

The Quran identifies twenty-five prophets by name, and the Sheikh discusses the most prominent among them. He dwells especially on the qualities that Allah required in His messengers: truthfulness (sidq), trustworthiness (amanah), communication of the message (tabligh), and intelligence (fatanah). These qualities are affirmed by the Quran and the authentic hadith and are the basis for trusting the revelation they delivered.

A central point of this section is that Muhammad, peace be upon him, is the Seal of the Prophets (khatam al-nabiyyin), and no prophet or messenger will come after him. This means that the Quran and his authenticated Sunnah are the final and complete guidance for all of humanity until the Day of Judgment. Al-Uthaymin connects this to the obligation of following the Prophet's example in every area of life and warns against any claim of new prophethood after him.

Belief in the messengers also requires not distinguishing between them in terms of belief — a Muslim must affirm all the prophets sent by Allah, as stated in the Quran: 'We do not differentiate between any of His messengers' (2:285). However, the messengers had different ranks with Allah, with Ibrahim and Muhammad holding the highest distinctions as the two ulul-azm (possessors of firm resolve) particularly emphasized in Islamic theology.

The fifth pillar — belief in the Last Day (al-yawm al-akhir) — encompasses everything that will occur after death: the questioning in the grave, the punishment and comfort of the barzakh (the intermediate state), the blowing of the trumpet, the resurrection, the gathering, the reckoning, the scales, the bridge over Hellfire (as-sirat), intercession (shafa'ah), and the final abode in either Paradise or Hellfire. Al-Uthaymin treats each of these in turn.

He stresses that these are matters of the unseen that must be believed as the Quran and Sunnah describe them, without rationalistic reinterpretation. He specifically addresses the reality of the punishment of the grave and its comfort, affirming them against those who deny them. He also discusses the major signs of the Hour — including the Dajjal (Antichrist), the descent of Isa ibn Maryam, the emergence of Gog and Magog, and the rise of the sun from the west — drawing on the authentic hadith literature.

The chapter emphasizes that consistent awareness of the Last Day is a spiritual anchor: a person who remembers the resurrection, the reckoning, and the eternal consequences of his deeds will be motivated to worship sincerely, to avoid sin, and to concern himself with what truly matters.
title_id: Pilar Keempat dan Kelima: Iman kepada Para Rasul dan Hari Akhir
content_id:
Al-Utsaimin membahas pilar keempat — iman kepada para rasul — dengan pertama-tama menetapkan keniscayaan kenabian. Karena akal manusia terbatas dan manusia cenderung mengikuti hawa nafsu, Allah dalam rahmat-Nya mengutus para rasul untuk menyampaikan petunjuk. Beliau mencatat bahwa para rasul dipilih oleh Allah, bukan mendaulat diri sendiri, dan diberi perlindungan ilahi dari dosa-dosa besar dalam menunaikan misi mereka (meskipun tidak dari keseluruhan pengalaman manusiawi, karena mereka adalah manusia yang makan, tidur, sakit, dan meninggal).

Al-Qur'an menyebutkan dua puluh lima nabi dengan nama, dan Syaikh membahas kewajiban iman kepada setiap nabi yang disebutkan dalam Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah, serta iman secara umum kepada semua nabi yang tidak disebutkan namanya. Beliau menjelaskan perbedaan antara nabi (yang menerima wahyu namun tidak diperintahkan untuk menyebarkannya secara luas) dan rasul (yang menerima wahyu dan diperintahkan untuk menyampaikannya kepada suatu kaum atau umat tertentu), meskipun penggunaan kedua istilah ini tumpang tindih dalam beberapa konteks.

Iman kepada Muhammad ﷺ sebagai rasul terakhir mendapat penekanan khusus. Al-Utsaimin menjelaskan bahwa iman kepada kerasulan Muhammad ﷺ mencakup: menerima semua yang beliau kabarkan, menaati semua perintahnya, menjauhi semua yang ia larang, dan tidak menyembah Allah kecuali dengan cara yang beliau syariatkan. Ini menjadikan Sunnah — praktik dan ajaran Nabi ﷺ yang tervalidasi — sumber otoritas agama yang wajib diikuti di samping Al-Qur'an.

Pilar kelima — iman kepada Hari Akhir — mencakup serangkaian keyakinan yang saling terkait: kematian dan kehidupan di kubur (fitnah kubur, nikmat dan azab kubur), kebangkitan (ba'ts), pengumpulan (hasyr), penghitungan amal (hisab), timbangan amal (mizan), jembatan (sirath), syafaat (syafa'at), surga (jannah), dan neraka (naar). Al-Utsaimin menguraikan masing-masing dari ini dengan merujuk pada teks-teks Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah yang sahih yang menetapkan realitasnya.

Penekanan pada kehidupan kubur adalah penting karena ini sering diabaikan dalam presentasi-presentasi Islam yang disederhanakan. Al-Utsaimin menjelaskan berdasarkan hadis-hadis sahih bahwa setelah kematian, roh seseorang mengalami kondisi baik dalam kubur (untuk orang-orang beriman) atau kesulitan (untuk orang-orang kafir dan pelaku maksiat berat), dan bahwa ini adalah tahap pertama dari perjalanan menuju Hari Akhir yang sesungguhnya.

Iman kepada Hari Akhir memiliki pengaruh transformatif terhadap kehidupan seseorang ketika sungguh-sungguh dipegang. Seseorang yang benar-benar percaya bahwa ia akan berdiri di hadapan Allah dan mempertanggungjawabkan setiap amalnya akan memperlakukan kehidupan dunia dengan cara yang berbeda dari seseorang yang tidak memiliki keyakinan ini. Al-Utsaimin menekankan bahwa salah satu tanda lemahnya iman adalah menghabiskan kehidupan mengejar hal-hal yang tidak bertahan setelah kematian sambil mengabaikan persiapan untuk apa yang akan bertahan selamanya.
content_ar: Al-Uthaymin addresses the رابعاً pillar — الاعتقاد in the الرسل — by أولاً establishing the necessity of prophethood. بما أن human العقل is limited and human beings are inclined للاتباع desires, الله in His الرحمة sent الرسل to convey الهداية. He notes that the الرسل were chosen by الله, not self-appointed, and were given divine protection from major الذنوب in the fulfilment of their mission (though not from the full range of human experiences, as they were men who ate, slept, fell ill, and died). The القرآن الكريم identifies twenty-five الأنبياء by name, and the Sheikh discusses the most prominent among them. He dwells ولا سيما on the qualities that الله required in His الرسل: truthfulness (sidq), trustworthiness (amanah), communication of the message (tabligh), and intelligence (fatanah). These qualities are affirmed by the القرآن الكريم and the authentic الحديث and are the basis for trusting the الوحي they delivered. A المحوري point of this الفصل is that محمد ﷺ, ﷺ, is the Seal of the الأنبياء (khatam al-nabiyyin), and no النبي or messenger will come بعد him. This means that the القرآن الكريم and his authenticated السنة are the final and complete الهداية for all of humanity حتى the يوم القيامة. Al-Uthaymin connects this to the الالتزام of following النبي ﷺ's example in every area of الحياة and warns against any claim of new prophethood بعد him. الاعتقاد in the الرسل أيضاً requires not distinguishing between them in terms of الاعتقاد — a مسلم must affirm all the الأنبياء sent by الله, as stated in the القرآن الكريم: 'We do not differentiate between any of His الرسل' (2:285). غير أن, the الرسل had different ranks with الله, with إبراهيم ﷺ and محمد ﷺ holding the highest distinctions as the two ulul-azm (possessors of firm resolve) particularly emphasized in Islamic theology. The خامساً pillar — الاعتقاد in the Last Day (al-yawm al-akhir) — encompasses everything that will occur بعد الموت: the questioning in the القبر, the العقاب and comfort of the barzakh (the intermediate state), the blowing of the trumpet, the البعث, the gathering, the reckoning, the scales, the bridge over النار (as-الصراط), intercession (shafa'ah), and the final abode in either الجنة or النار. Al-Uthaymin treats each of these in turn. He stresses that these are matters of the unseen that must be believed as the القرآن الكريم and السنة describe them, without rationalistic reinterpretation. He specifically addresses the reality of the العقاب of the القبر and its comfort, affirming them against those who deny them. He أيضاً discusses the major signs of the Hour — بما في ذلك the الدجال (المسيح الدجال), the descent of عيسى ibn Maryam, the emergence of Gog and Magog, and the rise of the sun from the west — drawing on the authentic الحديث literature.
